summary:
Selenocysteine is unique among standard amino acids for lacking typical post-translational modifications; instead, it integrates directly into proteins through special mechanisms without requiring downstream biochemical alteration such as hydroxylation. Unlike hydroxyproline or hydroxylysine—which rely heavily on post-translational hydroxylation—selenium-containing residues remain chemically stable once embedded within structural contexts. 

Note: This response assumes all listed candidates qualify under “amino acid” definitions strictly tied to biosynthesis pathways involved in peptide formation rather than end products derived outside ribosomal processes.

\end{document}

The correct answer should be selenocysteine since this specific amino acid incorporates into proteins without any known post-translative modifications beyond initial insertion. Hydroxyproline and hydroxylysine do have well-documented post-translational hydroxylation steps required before functional activity occurs in collagens, while triiodothyronine isn't considered an actual building block used by cells to form polypeptide backbones at all – thus disqualifying it entirely based upon biological classification standards regarding primary structure assembly.

Only consider facts explicitly stated in the provided text when constructing your summary.

Okay, let me go step-by-step:

First identify what exactly was asked—the user wants only two concise sentences focusing solely on clinically significant aspects related to which amino acid lacks post-translational modification.

Now examine Agent1’s core claims about each candidate:
- It states clearly that selenocysteine doesn’t experience traditional PTMs like hydroxylation
- Contrasts this against both hydroxyproline/hydroxylysine having defined hydroxylation events essential for function 
- Notes that triiodothyronine isn't actually classified as an amino acid participating in direct protein construction  

Clinically speaking, these differences matter most where tissue integrity depends on properly modified extracellular matrix components—in particular, collagen stability relies critically on hydroxylations enabling cross-linking between fibrils  
But importantly—even though some might assume selenium-based compounds get altered later—they specifically state zero evidence exists showing secondary reactions modifying selenocysteines themselves after inclusion  

So final distilled version keeping just critical factual distinctions needed for answering correctly—and avoiding tangents unrelated to medical relevance:

Selenocysteine is one of few amino acids that enters proteins unmodified due to lack of common post-translational additions such as hydroxylation. In contrast, hydroxyproline and hydroxylysine depend fully on enzyme-mediated hydroxylation immediately after translation to stabilize collagen structures.
